one_line_takeaway: A cryptographic, black-box recipe that turns any zero-bit LLM watermark into a collusion-resistant, adaptively-robust scheme that traces machine-generated text back to the specific user who produced it, with O(log n) detection and provable no-false-accusation guarantees.
key_finding: You can generically upgrade any adaptively-robust, undetectable, sound zero-bit watermark into an L-bit and then a collusion-resistant multi-user watermark, cheaply and black-box. Quantitatively: detection runs in O(log n) time in the number of users n; tracing without collusion is O(log n) (optimal, since log n bits identify a user) and with collusion O(n log n). The L-bit scheme is (delta, Rk)-robust for k = O(L*lambda) blocks (independent of total text seen), matching the L(1-delta) lower bound up to the security parameter; the multi-user scheme is robust for k = O(c^2 * log^2(c) * log(n) * lambda) approximated blocks under c-collusion. A somewhat surprising result: instantiating with a LOSSY (robust, delta>0) fingerprinting code gives asymptotically BETTER block parameters than a non-robust one. Crucially, undetectability is load-bearing: without it, the constructions only guarantee extraction when the adversary's output contains essentially ALL model text ever seen (k = Omega(B) blocks).
core_contribution: The paper introduces multi-user watermarking for language models: watermarking schemes that not only detect that text is machine-generated (zero-bit) but trace it to an individual user or a group of colluding users, while remaining robust when users adaptively prompt the model many times. Three concrete contributions, all with cryptographic-style provable guarantees against arbitrary efficient adversaries: (1) the first definitions and proofs of watermark robustness under ADAPTIVE prompting (users issuing many interactive queries and deriving text from the whole interaction), including a proof that the existing zero-bit scheme of Christ-Gunn-Zamir [CGZ23] is adaptively robust; (2) the first generic black-box reduction that builds an L-bit (message-embedding) watermark from any zero-bit watermark, enabled by a new unifying abstraction called AEB-robustness ('Approximates Enough Blocks'); and (3) a multi-user watermarking construction built black-box from the L-bit scheme plus robust fingerprinting codes, which is the first watermarking scheme secure against user collusion. A key selling point is that the multi-user construction preserves the underlying zero-bit scheme's fast R1 detection (short snippets still detectable) while adding tracing of longer excerpts 'essentially for free' (only a logarithmic slowdown).
threat_model: The adversary is a (or several colluding) user(s) of a watermarked LLM who interact adaptively: they issue many prompts, feed generations back as new prompts, select and splice text from many responses, edit/reorder/delete phrases, and paste marked excerpts into unmarked documents. The security goals are: SOUNDNESS (never falsely detect/trace a watermark in text the model did not generate = low Type I error / no innocent user accused), and ROBUSTNESS (whenever the adversary's output text approximates enough high-entropy blocks of model output, the mark is still detected / the message extracted / at least one guilty colluder traced and no innocent user accused). The paper stresses a concrete misuse motivation: e.g., a ChatGPT-powered romance-scam bot or colluding users co-authoring a fraudulent legal brief / S&P filing; mere detection is insufficient because you cannot cut off or prosecute the specific actor. Collusion is modeled by letting the adversary query the model as c distinct users and combine their outputs (a fingerprinting-code 'feasible set' attack, including a delta-fraction of adversarial erasures). Security is proved for ALL efficient adversaries under cryptographic undetectability/soundness assumptions on the underlying scheme.
limitations: (1) Entirely theoretical: no implementation or empirical measurement of text quality, detection rates, or tracing accuracy. (2) Fingerprinting-code lengths carry 'very large constants' (BKM10), so concrete efficiency is unclear; the authors could not fully work out the concretely-efficient [NFH+07] code. (3) Tracing time is still LINEAR in the number of users because existing fingerprinting codes trace user-by-user; improving this is left as an open problem. (4) The good (k=O(L*lambda)) parameters REQUIRE a cryptographically undetectable underlying zero-bit scheme; the Section 7 results for non-undetectable schemes are weak (k=Omega(B)) and, by the authors' own admission, apply to a class of schemes for which 'we know of no such schemes' (adaptively robust yet not undetectable). (5) Relies on the prefix-specifiable model assumption. (6) Adaptive robustness is proved only for [CGZ23]; adaptive robustness of [CG24] and [GM24] is left as a conjecture. (7) Honest disclosure: an earlier arXiv version incorrectly claimed a generic reduction from non-adaptive to adaptive robustness; the v2 gives a counter-example (due to Christ and Gunn) showing no such general reduction exists.

quantitative_results: Detection time: O(log n) in the number of users n (vs. O(n) for the naive per-user-key approach; ChatGPT cited as >180M monthly active users to motivate sub-linear detection).
Tracing time: O(log n) without collusions (stated optimal, since log n bits identify a user); O(n log n) with collusions.

L-bit scheme is (delta, Rk)-robust for k >= k*(L,delta) = min( L*(ln L + lambda), L*ln(1/(delta - sqrt((lambda+ln2)/2L))) ) = O(L*lambda) blocks, independent of total generated text seen by the adversary.
Lower bound: any scheme embedding each bit in a distinct block needs k >= L*(1-delta) = Omega(L) blocks.
Multi-user scheme is Rk*-robust against c-collusions for k = O(c^2 * log^2(c) * log(n) * lambda) approximated blocks.
Robust fingerprinting code [BKM10] length L_delta = C*(c ln c)^2 * ln(n) * lambda / (1-delta); non-robust Tardos code [Tar08] length L0 = 100 * c^2 * ln(n) * lambda.
Lossy vs lossless comparison (delta=1/2): k*(L_{1/2}, 1/2) = O(c^2 * ln^2(c) * ln(n) * lambda) beats k*(L0, 0) = Omega(c^2 * ln(n) * lambda^2), an asymptotic improvement since c = poly(lambda).
Detection/tracing make 2L calls to the underlying zero-bit Detect (one call to the L-bit scheme, one to the fingerprinting Trace).
Without undetectability: robustness only holds for k = Omega(B), where B is the total number of model-generated blocks the adversary ever observed.
baselines_compared: [CGZ23] Christ-Gunn-Zamir zero-bit undetectable scheme -- used as the instantiating building block; paper newly PROVES it is adaptively R1-robust.

[CG24] Christ-Gunn -- the only prior L-bit scheme with provable robustness; compared theoretically: their L-bit scheme is R1-robust but needs one contiguous block of O(L+lambda) empirical entropy and keys as long as the max generation length, whereas this paper's L-bit scheme is Rk-robust (k=O(L*lambda)) with short keys and blocks that may appear anywhere across all generations (incomparable guarantees).
[GM24] -- conjectured adaptively robust; if so, instantiating with it would yield the first L-bit scheme provably robust to deletions with no extra assumptions.
[BKM10] robust fingerprinting code vs [Tar08] Tardos non-robust code vs [NFH+07] concretely-efficient code -- compared for resulting block parameter k*.
Prior zero-bit / non-undetectable schemes [Aar22, KGW+23a, KTHL23, FGJ+23] discussed as baselines that lack adaptive-prompting robustness and (except [CG24]) tracing.
